sound_type: wild laughing wuk-wuk-wuk -- rapid descending series of loud ringing notes, territorial declaration, primal scream through old-growth canopy, E=6 MAXIMUM
ecosystem: Old-growth forest -- Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce. Mature and old-growth conifer and mixed forest. The forest canopy as acoustic chamber.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Territorial. Solitary or in pairs.

coordinates.description: Reference for old-growth forest habitat in the central Cascades. Pileated Woodpeckers found in mature and old-growth conifer forest throughout the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: RETURN SPECIES from degree 54. Dryocopus pileatus -- same species, new context (laughing-call). Second consecutive Picidae (107-108). Degree 54 was general; degree 108 isolates the wild laughing call. E=6 on the species side -- MAXIMUM. E=6 on both sides simultaneously (S36 Veronica Lipgloss + SPS Pileated laughing) -- first time ever.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 5 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dryocopus from Greek drys ('tree') + kopos ('beating') -- 'tree-beater.' pileatus from Latin pileatus ('capped, crested') -- referring to the prominent red crest. The Pileated Woodpecker: the crested tree-beater.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dryocopus returns from degree 54. The largest North American woodpecker genus. The Pileated is the only surviving member in North America (the Ivory-billed Woodpecker, D. principalis/Campephilus principalis, is likely extinct).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Second consecutive (107-108). After the Corvidae septet (Passeriformes 100-106), two Picidae (Piciformes 107-108).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae continues for a second consecutive degree. After Acorn Woodpecker (107), the Pileated returns (108). Two consecutive Picidae after seven consecutive Corvidae.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 800-3,500 Hz. Wild laughing call -- loud, ringing, descending series. Low fundamental with strong harmonics.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,000-2,500 Hz. The ringing quality comes from the low fundamental resonating with the old-growth canopy.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Wild laughing call: 1,000-3,000 Hz, rapid descending series, ringing. Drumming: low-frequency resonant hammering on large dead trees.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Wild laughing call: 3-6 seconds. A rapid series of 10-20+ notes that accelerates and descends. Carries hundreds of meters through forest.
song_metrics.note_structure: WILD LAUGHING WUK: Rapid series of loud, ringing, slightly descending notes -- wuk wuk wuk wuk wuk wuk. Accelerates slightly. The 'laughing' quality comes from the rhythm and descending pitch. Compared to the Woody Woodpecker laugh (inspired by a related species).
song_metrics.note_types: Wild laughing call (primary, territorial/contact), drumming (territorial -- powerful, resonant, slow roll on large dead trees), contact calls (softer wuk notes between pair members). The wild laughing call is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single descending series. The wild laughing call is one continuous burst of rapid notes that accelerates and descends. No repeated phrases. Each call is a single explosive burst.
song_metrics.timbre: Ringing. Wild. Primal. The most powerful timbre of any PNW woodpecker. Deep fundamental with strong harmonics. The old-growth canopy adds reverb and resonance. The timbre is unmistakable.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6 -- MAXIMUM ENERGY. The wild laughing call is the loudest vocalization of any PNW woodpecker. Carries hundreds of meters through old-growth forest. The old-growth canopy amplifies and reverberates. Overwhelming in person.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid. The wuk-wuk-wuk accelerates slightly through the series. Faster than the Acorn Woodpecker's waka-waka-waka but with larger, more resonant notes.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND CONTACT. The wild laughing call declares territory and maintains long-distance contact between pair members. Given year-round but most frequently in spring (breeding season). The call announces: this forest is occupied.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal variation. The wild laughing call is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: WILD LAUGHING WUK-WUK-WUK -- the primal scream. E=6 MAXIMUM. Rapid descending ringing series echoing through old-growth forest. The most powerful vocalization of any PNW woodpecker. RETURN from degree 54 (general to laughing-call context). Energy escalation (E=5 to E=6). SECOND CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-108). E=6 ON BOTH SIDES (S36 Veronica Lipgloss + SPS Pileated laughing) -- first time in the engine. Forty-eighth consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Large woodpecker -- the largest in North America (with the possible exception of the likely-extinct Ivory-billed). Predominantly black with a bright red crest. White wing linings visible in flight. White stripe on face and neck.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 108: (1) LARGE SIZE -- crow-sized woodpecker, much larger than any other PNW woodpecker except possibly the extirpated Ivory-billed. (2) RED CREST -- prominent, triangular red crest on black head. (3) BLACK AND WHITE IN FLIGHT -- black body with bright white wing linings flash in undulating flight. (4) OLD-GROWTH HABITAT -- large woodpecker in mature/old-growth forest with the wild laughing call.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Males have a red moustache stripe; females have a black moustache stripe.
plumage.flight_pattern: Deep undulating flight. Black body with bright white wing linings conspicuous in flight. The large size and undulating pattern are diagnostic even at a distance.

physical_description.overall: Large woodpecker. Body length 40-49 cm. Wingspan 66-75 cm. Weight 250-400 g. Predominantly black with bright red triangular crest. White wing linings. White facial stripe. The largest woodpecker in North America.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 108: crow-sized black woodpecker with bright red crest in old-growth forest, giving a wild laughing wuk-wuk-wuk call. Unmistakable. Nothing else this large and dramatic in the PNW forest.
physical_description.field_id: Very large black woodpecker with red crest and white wing linings in mature/old-growth forest. Wild laughing call. If you hear a loud, ringing, rapid wuk-wuk-wuk-wuk echoing through old-growth -- it is a Pileated Woodpecker.

nodes.title: E=6 on Both Sides -- Maximum Energy Simultaneously
nodes.description: E=6 on both S36 (Veronica Lipgloss, punk/electronic) and SPS (Pileated Woodpecker laughing, old-growth) at degree 108. Maximum energy on both tracks simultaneously. First time in the engine. Unprecedented.

nodes.title: Wild Laughing Wuk-Wuk-Wuk -- The Primal Scream
nodes.description: Rapid descending series of loud ringing notes. E=6 MAXIMUM. Territorial declaration echoing through old-growth canopy. The most powerful vocalization of any PNW woodpecker. Overwhelming in person.

nodes.title: Pileated Returns -- General to Laughing-Call Context
nodes.description: First at degree 54 (general, E=5). Returns at degree 108 (laughing-call, E=6). 54-degree gap. Energy escalation. The wild laughing call isolated and elevated to maximum.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Picidae (107-108)
nodes.description: Acorn Woodpecker (107, oak savanna, E=5, colonial, waka-waka-waka) and Pileated laughing (108, old-growth, E=6, solitary, wuk-wuk-wuk). After the Corvidae septet, two Picidae with maximum contrast.

nodes.title: Old-Growth Forest -- Acoustic Chamber
nodes.description: Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce. The old-growth canopy amplifies and reverberates the Pileated's laughing call. The forest is an acoustic chamber. Shared habitat with Northern Spotted Owl (4) and Northern Flying Squirrel (82).

cross_references.relationship: RETURN! First at degree 54 (general context, E=5). Returns at degree 108 (laughing-call context, E=6). 54-degree gap. Energy escalation. The wild laughing call isolated and elevated to maximum.
SECOND CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-108). Acorn in oak savanna (E=5, colonial, waka-waka-waka). Pileated in old-growth (E=6, solitary, wuk-wuk-wuk). Maximum contrast in two degrees.
PICIDAE FAMILY. Engine's first woodpecker (degrees 21-22, wicka-wicka-wicka, E=4). Pileated laughing is the highest-energy woodpecker (E=6). Same family, maximum energy range.
OLD-GROWTH SHARED HABITAT. Both require old-growth forest. The Spotted Owl hunts in silence; the Pileated screams through the canopy. Two old-growth specialists, opposite acoustic strategies.

CAVITY CONNECTION. The Pileated excavates large cavities in old-growth trees. The Flying Squirrel uses old woodpecker cavities for nesting. Pileated is cavity creator; squirrel is cavity user. Community infrastructure.
